context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on neoliberalism vs the East Asian developmental state, and I’m kinda stuck on how to compare them. I think neoliberalism wants a limited state, but idk how to explain East Asia and civil society/labor clearly.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. You mention neoliberalism wants a 'limited state' — can you say more about what that actually means? What specifically should the state do, and not do, according to neoliberal thinking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic grasp of neoliberalism but needs to develop their thinking further. Rather than scaffolding the comparison for them, I'll use a Socratic question to get them to articulate what they already know about the neoliberal state, which is the logical starting point for the comparison the prompt asks for. This keeps the response bite-sized and puts the thinking work on the student.

exchanges.student: I think it means the state should mostly protect markets, like property rights and contracts, but not directly manage the economy? I’m not fully sure what counts as “too much” intervention tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable intuition. But why do neoliberals think direct state intervention in the economy is problematic in the first place? What's the underlying logic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition about neoliberalism. Rather than confirming or expanding on it myself, I should push them to sharpen their thinking by asking what the reasoning *behind* this limited role is — i.e., why neoliberals believe state intervention is harmful. This moves them toward a more complete understanding they can use in their reflection.

exchanges.student: I think the logic is that markets are supposed to allocate resources better than governments, and state officials can be inefficient or political. Is it basically that intervention distorts prices and incentives?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the core of it. Now, with that neoliberal logic in mind — how does what actually happened in East Asia (think South Korea, Taiwan, Japan) fit or challenge that picture?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated the core neoliberal logic quite well — market efficiency, price signals, and the risks of government failure. I should affirm this is solid thinking without over-praising, then pivot them toward East Asia, which is the next logical step in the comparison the prompt requires.

exchanges.student: It kinda challenges it a lot, right? East Asian states weren’t passive at all — I think they guided industry, protected some sectors, and pushed exports, but I’m fuzzy on the exact contrast.
exchanges.tutor: You're right that there's a strong contrast. Can you think of a specific thing East Asian states did — like a concrete policy or mechanism — that a neoliberal state would consider off-limits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track recognizing the contrast but is being vague. I should push them to be more specific about what the East Asian state actually did, since their reflection will need concrete substance. A targeted question about a specific mechanism will help them move from vague to precise without me giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe stuff like industrial policy, subsidized credit, or picking “strategic” industries? Ngl I always mix up whether that’s just protectionism or something more specific.
exchanges.tutor: That's worth thinking about. What do you think is the difference between just protecting an industry from competition versus actively steering it toward a developmental goal? Does the state's *intent* or *conditionality* matter here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a genuinely useful distinction between protectionism and industrial policy. Rather than resolving it for them, I should ask them to think about what differentiates the two — this will sharpen their analytical thinking and help them write a more precise reflection. This is a productive detour that directly serves the assignment.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think conditionality matters — like not just protecting firms forever, but giving support if they meet export or productivity goals. So it’s more like disciplined state guidance than simple blanket protection, right?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction and it'll serve you well in your reflection. Now, here's a harder question: given that East Asian states were so interventionist and 'disciplined,' what was the role of labor, civil society, or democracy in that model? Were workers and citizens active partners in this process?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on a key concept — disciplined support with performance conditions — which is central to the developmental state literature. This is good progress. Now I should pivot them toward the third part of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet: the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. This is where many students have gaps and it's essential to the reflection.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing not really equal partners tbh — like growth may have come with labor repression or weak participation. But idk how to say that without oversimplifying East Asia as just authoritarian.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're sensing is actually important to hold onto — don't flatten it. So here's the question: if growth happened alongside constrained labor and limited democracy, what does that imply about *who bore the costs* of the East Asian miracle? And does that complicate how we evaluate it as a development model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical awareness — they sense the tension between authoritarianism and the complexity of East Asian cases. This is exactly the kind of nuance the reflection needs. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to think about what this tension means for evaluating the East Asian model, which will help them write a more sophisticated argument.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think it means workers prob bore a lot of the costs through wage restraint, weak unions, or limited political voice. So the “miracle” looks less neutral since growth happened, but the benefits and sacrifices weren’t shared evenly.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong analytical point. You've now covered most of the prompt well. But there's one part you haven't addressed yet — the prompt asks you to consider state intervention 'in the context of new institutionalist theories.' What do you know about that, and how do you think it connects to what we've discussed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a strong critical point about distributional costs in the East Asian model. This is excellent progress. We've now covered neoliberalism, the developmental state, and civil society/labor — three of the four parts of the prompt. The fourth part about new institutionalist theories hasn't been touched yet. I should nudge them toward it since it's explicitly part of the prompt and the reflection would be incomplete without it.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit lost on new institutionalism rn. I think it’s about institutions shaping incentives, so maybe the point is that effective development needs capable institutions, not just “more” or “less” state?
exchanges.tutor: That's a decent starting intuition. So how might that reframe the neoliberal vs. East Asia debate you've already worked through? Instead of arguing about *how much* state intervention, what might a new institutionalist say is the more important question?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition about new institutionalism. Rather than elaborating further, I should connect it back to the East Asian case and the neoliberal contrast they've already developed, so they can see how it adds a third lens to their reflection. This keeps momentum toward completing the argument without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe the bigger question is what kind of state capacity and institutions exist, not just the amount of intervention. So I think I should argue that East Asia worked partly because intervention was embedded in strong institutions, discipline, and state-business coordination, not just bc the state was big.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong synthesis — you've essentially built the spine of your argument. Now it's time to move from thinking to writing. Take what you've worked out in this conversation and try drafting your reflection. What's your opening move — how do you plan to frame the piece from the start?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now synthesized all the key elements of the prompt into a coherent argument: neoliberal state vs. developmental state, labor/civil society costs, and the new institutionalist lens. This is good intellectual progress. We've covered all four parts of the prompt across roughly 9 exchanges. I should now push the student to move from discussion to writing, since the reflection is the actual deliverable and they haven't started drafting yet.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic dialogue across all 10 turns. Key ideas were consistently elicited from the student rather than given by the tutor; the tutor asked probing follow-up questions at each step, built on student responses, and guided the student to a strong synthesis without ever providing submission-ready content. Responses were concise and well-calibrated to the student's level. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave final answers or near-submission-ready content; every substantive insight (neoliberal logic, conditionality vs. protectionism, labor costs, new institutionalism) was drawn out of the student through questioning. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the conditionality distinction in Turn 6, the labor-cost point in Turn 8) and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., probing new institutionalism in Turn 9 only after the student flagged uncertainty). Under 1.3, the tutor provided process-level feedback, notably telling the student in Turn 7 to 'hold onto' the tension rather than flatten it, which is methodological guidance. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misframing, and no off-topic drift. Communication was bite-sized and supportive throughout, with no grading language.
